items.title: Practice Passage: The Quiet Power of Default Choices
items.summary: A reading-comprehension practice passage on behavioural economics and default options, with approach guidance on tracking the argument's structure and handling inference and tone questions.
items.body:
Classical economic theory assumes that people weigh costs and benefits and choose whatever maximises their welfare. Behavioural economists have spent four decades documenting how far real decisions stray from this ideal. One of the most striking findings concerns defaults: the option a person receives if they do nothing at all. When organ-donor registration requires citizens to opt in, participation tends to be modest; when registration is the default and citizens must opt out, participation is routinely far higher, even though the effort required to switch is trivial in both cases.

Why do defaults exert such force? Three explanations dominate the literature. First, inertia: changing an option demands attention, and attention is scarce. Second, implied endorsement: people read the default as a recommendation from a knowledgeable designer, so sticking with it feels safe. Third, loss aversion: once a default is mentally treated as the status quo, abandoning it feels like a loss, and losses loom larger than equivalent gains.

Critics worry that default-setting shades into manipulation. If a firm enrols customers in a costly add-on by default, the same psychology that helps savers can quietly drain wallets. Defenders respond that some default always exists; the only real question is whether it is chosen thoughtfully or carelessly. They argue that transparency, easy reversal, and alignment with what most choosers would themselves want distinguish a legitimate nudge from a trap.

The deeper lesson is that choice architecture is unavoidable. Every form, menu, and enrolment screen embeds assumptions about human attention, and pretending otherwise simply hands the design decision to chance.

How to approach this passage: identify the central claim early, namely that defaults powerfully shape behaviour, and then track the structure of phenomenon, explanations, objection, rebuttal, and conclusion. Inference questions will probe the three explanations, so keep them distinct in your head. A tone question should register the author as analytical and mildly sympathetic to nudging, not as a neutral reporter. For a main-idea question, prefer the option that covers both the power of defaults and the debate about their legitimacy, rather than one that mentions only organ donation.

items.title: Practice Passage: Circular Migration and the City That Plans for Flows
items.summary: A practice passage on urbanisation and circular migration, with guidance on mapping the claim-complication-debate structure and avoiding extreme answer options.
items.body:
Urbanisation is often narrated as a simple story of pull: cities offer jobs, so villages empty. Yet the texture of migration in many developing regions complicates this account. A large share of movement is circular rather than permanent. Workers travel to construction sites or factories for months, remit earnings home, and return for sowing seasons or festivals. Households thus straddle two economies at once, using the city as a wage machine and the village as insurance, childcare, and old-age security.

This circularity has consequences that planners frequently miss. Census instruments designed to count settled residents undercount a floating population that may form a sizeable fraction of a city's workforce. Housing policy aimed at permanent family accommodation does little for single migrants who want cheap, flexible beds near worksites. Transport budgets follow registered residents, while peripheral settlements where migrants actually cluster remain underserved.

Some scholars argue that circular migration is a transitional phase that will fade as urban incomes rise and families relocate wholesale, as happened in many industrialised countries. Others counter that high urban housing costs, insecure informal work, and strong rural land ties make circularity a durable equilibrium rather than a way station. If the second view is right, the policy task is not to accelerate permanent settlement but to make mobility itself less punishing: portable entitlements, rental housing for short stays, and identity documents that travel with the worker.

The disagreement matters because it determines whether cities should plan for residents or for flows. A city built only for the settled will keep treating its most mobile workers as invisible.

How to approach this passage: the passage is organised as claim, complication, consequences, and a two-sided scholarly debate, ending with the author leaning towards planning for mobility. Expect a question on why circular migrants are undercounted and another on the difference between the two scholarly positions. Avoid extreme answer options that say circular migration is always permanent or always temporary; the author treats the question as open while signalling that policy should not assume settlement. The final sentence is a strong cue for any question about the author's purpose.

items.title: Practice Passage: Can a Single Experiment Kill a Theory?
items.summary: A philosophy-of-science practice passage on falsifiability and its refinement, with guidance on the thesis-objection-refinement structure and the function of examples.
items.body:
What separates science from non-science? One influential twentieth-century answer holds that the mark of a scientific claim is falsifiability: a genuine theory must forbid certain observations, so that the world can in principle show it to be wrong. Astrology, on this view, fails not because its predictions are false but because they are so elastic that no outcome could ever count against them. Science earns its authority by sticking its neck out.

Attractive as it is, the criterion runs into trouble when confronted with scientific practice. Theories are never tested alone. To check a prediction about a planet's orbit, one needs auxiliary assumptions about telescopes, light, and the absence of unknown bodies. When the prediction fails, logic alone cannot say whether the theory or an auxiliary assumption is at fault. Historically, scientists have often rescued good theories by blaming the auxiliaries, and sometimes that stubbornness proved fruitful, as when an anomalous orbit led to the discovery of a new planet rather than the abandonment of celestial mechanics.

Later philosophers therefore shifted attention from individual theories to research traditions. A tradition is healthy, they suggested, when its adjustments lead to novel predictions that succeed, and degenerate when adjustments merely patch holes after the fact. This reframing preserves the falsificationist instinct that science must risk failure, while acknowledging that judging a theory is a matter of track record over time, not a single decisive experiment.

The practical upshot is humility in both directions: a failed test rarely kills a theory instantly, and a confirmed test never proves one finally.

How to approach this passage: this is a classic thesis-objection-refinement structure. The first paragraph states the falsifiability criterion, the second raises the objection that predictions depend on auxiliary assumptions, and the third presents the refined view. Questions will test whether you can state the objection precisely and whether you see that the final view modifies rather than abandons falsificationism. Beware of options claiming the author rejects falsifiability entirely; the author refines it. The planet example supports the objection, so a question may ask what function it serves in the argument, and the answer is illustration of a successful rescue, not proof that theories are never wrong.

items.title: Practice Passage: Old Panics, New Feedback Loops
items.summary: A technology-and-society practice passage on recommendation systems and public discourse, with guidance on locating the author's nuanced stance and the passage's conceptual core.
items.body:
Every era of communication technology has provoked anxieties about what it does to public life. The printing press was accused of flooding readers with pamphlets no authority could vet; the telegraph was blamed for privileging speed over reflection. Today's worry centres on recommendation systems, which decide what billions of people read and watch by predicting what will hold their attention. The charge is that optimising for engagement systematically favours the sensational over the sober, fragmenting audiences into mutually unintelligible enclaves.

The evidence, however, is messier than the rhetoric. Studies of browsing behaviour find that most people's media diets are more varied than the enclave thesis predicts, partly because the same systems that serve outrage also serve serendipity. Some researchers argue that polarisation is driven less by what platforms show people than by what people already want, with technology amplifying demand rather than creating it. Others reply that amplification at scale is precisely the problem: a preference that was once private and occasional becomes a constant, algorithmically reinforced habit.

What distinguishes the present moment from earlier panics may be less the content than the feedback loop. Printers could not watch readers' eyes and adjust the next pamphlet in milliseconds. Modern systems can, and they tune themselves to fine-grained behavioural signals that users do not consciously endorse. A person may click on alarming material while sincerely wishing for calmer fare, and the system obeys the click, not the wish. The gap between revealed and reflective preference, long a curiosity in economics, has become an engineering parameter.

Whether regulation, redesign, or changed user habits will close that gap remains an open question, but framing it accurately is the first step.

How to approach this passage: notice that the author neither endorses nor dismisses the alarm; the stance is that the debate is unsettled but the genuinely new element is the feedback loop between behaviour and curation. The contrast between revealed and reflective preference is the conceptual core and the most likely inference target. The historical examples in the opening contextualise current concerns rather than trivialise them, so an easy trap option will claim the author thinks today's worries are mere repetition of old panics. Reject it.

items.title: Reading Comprehension Strategy: Map the Passage, Not the Words
items.summary: An explainer on a structure-first method for reading comprehension: paragraph mapping, reflexes for each question type, elimination discipline, and a daily practice routine.
items.body:
Reading comprehension is less a test of reading than a test of mapping. The examiner wants to know whether you can identify what an author claims, how the claim is supported, where rival views appear, and what attitude the author takes towards them. Speed matters, but speed comes from structure, not from skimming.

Read the passage once at a steady pace with one goal: assign a job to every paragraph. Typical jobs include introducing a phenomenon, illustrating it, raising an objection, conceding a point, refuting a rival, and concluding. If you can give each paragraph a five-word label, you have understood enough to attempt every question, even before you remember any detail.

Question types call for different reflexes. Main-idea questions reward the option that covers the whole passage at the right altitude, neither a single example nor a vague generality. Inference questions ask what must be true given the text, so reject options that merely could be true or that import outside knowledge. Tone questions are settled by verbs and qualifiers; words like merely, remarkably, or so-called reveal stance more reliably than topic. Detail questions are the only type where you should return to the passage and verify the exact line.

Elimination is your sharpest tool, and four traps recur across exams. Extreme options convert the author's measured claim into always or never. Out-of-scope options discuss something the passage never addresses. Distortions reverse a relationship, swapping cause for effect or claim for concession. Half-true options begin accurately and smuggle in a false ending, so read every option to its last word.

Finally, build the habit deliberately. Take one passage daily, summarise each paragraph in the margin in your own words, attempt the questions untimed, then review every option, including the ones you got right, and articulate why each wrong option is wrong. Accuracy built this way survives exam pressure; speed acquired without it does not. Over several weeks, reduce your time per passage gradually rather than all at once, and track which trap type fools you most often so your revision targets a specific weakness rather than a vague sense of difficulty.

items.title: Parajumbles Strategy: Openers, Mandatory Pairs, and Closers
items.summary: An explainer on solving parajumbles by spotting opening sentences, locking mandatory pairs through pronouns and connectors, and verifying the final sequence for flow.
items.body:
Parajumbles give you four to six sentences in scrambled order and ask you to reconstruct the original paragraph. The underlying skill is coherence: seeing how writers link sentences through reference, logic, and time.

Begin by hunting for the opener. A strong opening sentence introduces its subject independently, usually with a full noun phrase rather than a pronoun. Sentences that start with pronouns such as he, this, or such, with contrast markers such as however or but, or with additives such as also and moreover are rarely openers, because each leans on something said earlier.

Next, lock mandatory pairs. A pronoun must follow the noun it points to. A sentence containing the committee usually follows one that introduced a committee. An example signposted by for instance follows the general claim it illustrates. Cause precedes effect, problem precedes solution, and chronology runs forward unless the writer clearly flags a flashback. Two or three such links typically fix most of the sequence.

Then identify the closer. Concluding sentences generalise, evaluate, or state consequences, often with markers like thus, ultimately, or in the long run. They rarely introduce new actors or open fresh questions.

When answer options are given, eliminate aggressively: reject every option that violates a mandatory pair, and you will often be left with a single survivor. When no options are given, as in type-in-the-answer formats, build the chain link by link and then read your final order silently from start to finish; a correct sequence flows without jolts, while a wrong one usually stumbles at exactly one joint.

Two errors cause most lost marks. The first is forcing an order based on topic similarity alone; two sentences about the same subject are not necessarily adjacent. The second is ignoring grammar cues such as tense shifts and singular-plural agreement, which often settle ambiguous links. In practice sessions, do not stop at the right answer. Write down which specific cue settled each link, whether a pronoun, a connector, or a time marker. Over time you will start spotting these chains within seconds, which is what separates consistent scorers from lucky ones.

items.title: Para-Summary Strategy: Compress First, Then Compare
items.summary: An explainer on para-summary questions: write your own one-line compression before reading options, then eliminate narrow, broad, distorted, and added choices.
items.body:
Para-summary questions present a dense paragraph and four candidate one-line summaries. Your job is to pick the option that restates the author's central point at the right scope, with the author's emphasis and qualifications intact.

The reliable method has three steps. First, read the paragraph and identify its core assertion, the sentence the rest of the paragraph exists to support, explain, or qualify. Second, before looking at any option, compress that assertion into your own single line, including any condition the author insists on. Third, match your line against the options and eliminate those that deviate. Writing your own version first inoculates you against persuasive-sounding wrong options.

Four traps account for nearly all errors. Narrow options summarise one example or one limb of the argument and ignore the rest. Broad options inflate the paragraph into a sweeping claim about life, society, or human nature that the author never makes. Distortions flip a relationship: a paragraph saying growth enables welfare is summarised as welfare enabling growth, or a conditional claim is rendered as an unconditional one. Additions smuggle in advice, predictions, or judgements absent from the text; a paragraph that describes a problem is not summarised by an option that recommends a solution.

Pay special attention to logical connectors and hedges. If the paragraph turns on although, unless, only if, or largely, the correct summary must preserve that structure. An option that drops largely and asserts the claim absolutely has changed the meaning, however close its vocabulary appears to the original.

Finally, verify the surviving option word by word. A summary can be ninety per cent faithful and still wrong in its last phrase, so the final check matters as much as the first reading. In daily practice, attempt three or four such questions, and for each wrong option write one phrase naming its flaw: narrow, broad, distorted, or added. Building this vocabulary of errors turns a vague feeling that an option is off into a fast, repeatable decision you can trust under exam pressure.
